coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional chitosan non-woven fabrics modified with terylene carbon dots for selective detection and efficient adsorption of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Tianwu
Zeng, Lifan
Li, Yaqin
Wu, Yajing
Zhu, Zece
Zhang, Yanbo
Tian, Di
Gao, Chao
Li, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A novel terylene carbon dots (TCDs) were successfully synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method, and further modified onto chitosan non-woven fabrics (CNF) for selective detection and effective removal of Cr(VI). Compared with TCDs, chitosan non-woven fabrics modified by carbon dots (CNF-TCDs3) exhibited higher selectivity and lower detection limit for Cr(VI) (The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.15 mg/L). In addition, CNF-TCDs3 showed a rapid and effective removal of Cr(VI), and still maintain a high relative removal rate (above 92%) after five times recirculation. The adsorption equilibrium could be reached in only 8 min, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 203 mg/g. The adsorption process conformed to the Langmuir model, and the fitting correlation is R2 = 0.99. The fluorescence response and efficient adsorption mechanisms of the CNF-TCDs3 toward Cr(VI) were mainly attributed to the internal filtering effect and the amino and hydroxyl groups in chitosan. Significantly, this functionalized fabrics CNF-TCDs3 were successfully applied to semi-quantitatively detect on a smartphone-integrated-device and efficiently remove Cr(VI) in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Terylene
Chitosan non-woven fabric
Cr(VI)
Detection
Adsorption

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)TEM images and (b) DLS number mean size distribution curves of TCDs; (c) Different concentrations of TCDs modified CNF and its fluorescence images; (d) FTIR spectroscopy of TCDs, CNF and CNF-TCDs3; (e) SEM images of CNF-TCDs3; (f) XRD pattern of TCDs, CNF and CNF-TCDs3.

(a) The linear relationships between FL intensity of CNF-TCDs3 and the Cr(VI) concentration; (b) The linear relationships between FL brightness of CNF-TCDs3 and the Cr(VI) concentration; (c) The ratio of the fluorescence intensity F of CNF-TCDs3 in metal cations to the fluorescence intensity F0 in water F/F0 histogram; (d) The ratio of the fluorescence intensity F of CNF-TCDs3 in oxyanions to the fluorescence intensity F0 in water F/F0 histogram.

(a) The adsorption kinetics of CNF-TCDs3; (b) The adsorption isotherm curves of CNF-TCDs3 for Cr(VI); (c) Bar graph of adsorption and desorption capacity and cycle-index of CNF-TCDs3 adsorption Cr(VI); (d) The bright and dark field and before and after Cr(VI) adsorption of CNF-TCDs3.

(a) FTIR spectra before and after Cr(VI) adsorption of CNF-TCDs3; (b) The zeta potential diagram of CNF, TCDs and CNF-TCDs3; (c) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution spectra of (d) Cr 2p, (e) N 1 s, (f) O 1 s before and after Cr(VI) adsorption of CNF-TCDs3; (g) The scheme of adsorption mechanisms.

(a) Self-made fluorescence colorimetric semi-quantitative detection device; (b) Fluorescence dark field image of semi-quantitative detection card taken by smartphone; (c) The images before and after the solution is filtered through a simple device.
Schematic illustration of the detection and adsorption of Cr(VI) based on the TCDs modified CNF synthesized from terylene.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional chitosan non-woven fabrics modified with terylene carbon dots for selective detection and efficient adsorption of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430073, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing
Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430073, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel terylene carbon dots (TCDs) modified on chitosan non-woven fabrics (CNF) were successfully applied to semi-quantitatively detect on a smartphone-integrated-device and efficiently remove Cr(VI) in real samples.

A novel terylene carbon dots (TCDs) were successfully synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method, and further modified onto chitosan non-woven fabrics (CNF) for selective detection and effective removal of Cr(VI). Compared with TCDs, chitosan non-woven fabrics modified by carbon dots (CNF-TCDs3) exhibited higher selectivity and lower detection limit for Cr(VI) (The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.15 mg/L). In addition, CNF-TCDs3 showed a rapid and effective removal of Cr(VI), and still maintain a high relative removal rate (above 92%) after five times recirculation. The adsorption equilibrium could be reached in only 8 min, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 203 mg/g. The adsorption process conformed to the Langmuir model, and the fitting correlation is R2 = 0.99. The fluorescence response and efficient adsorption mechanisms of the CNF-TCDs3 toward Cr(VI) were mainly attributed to the internal filtering effect and the amino and hydroxyl groups in chitosan. Significantly, this functionalized fabrics CNF-TCDs3 were successfully applied to semi-quantitatively detect on a smartphone-integrated-device and efficiently remove Cr(VI) in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hexavalent chromium Cr(VI), one of the heavy metal series, are produced or discharged in industrial wastewater such as textiles and electroplating. The forms of Cr(VI) are mainly HCrO4 -, CrO4 2-, and Cr2O7 2- ions, which are carcinogenic and mutagenic [1]. Exposure to a certain amount of Cr(VI) is likely to cause cellular respiratory diseases, genetic defects, cancer, and even death [2–4]. Therefore, it is indispensable to detect and remove Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater.

In the past decades, the commonly detection methods for Cr(VI) are ultraviolet visible spectrophotometry (UV) [5], ion chromatography (IC) [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7], electrochemical analysis [8], and fluorescence detection [9]. Among which, the fluorescence (FL) detection has attracted more attentions due to its superior sensitivity, low cost, experimental convenience. Carbon dots (CDs) as a newly fluorescent nanomaterial have the advantages of green preparation, low toxicity, and good water-soluble compared with traditional organic fluorescent probes. CDs have been widely used to detect many metal ions such as Fe3+ [10,11], Cu2+ [12,13], Al3+ [14], Hg2+ [15,16], Pb2+ [17,18], and molecules such as glucose [19], ciprofloxacin [13], glutathione [20], etc. In addition, numerous studies proved that CDs could effectively detect Cr(VI) [21–28]. In usual, the carbon sources for synthesizing CDs to detect Cr(VI) are purchased pure substances and compounds. Nevertheless, finding an environmentally friendly and easily available carbon source are always the goal pursued by researchers. The detection of heavy metal pollutants is mostly sampled back to the laboratory and depends on large fluorescent instruments, how to achieve rapid detection on site is also a research hotspot for many researchers.

Recently, a large number of textile scraps and used clothes resulted in massive waste of resources and environmental pollution [29–31]. As a kind of textile, terylene is mainly composed of polyethylene terephthalate, which is formed by polycondensation of terephthalic acid and ethylene glycol [32]. However, the conventional chemical and physical recycling methods are complicated and costly [33], and the presence of dyes made the procession more difficult. Landfill or incineration, the common treatments, have caused more pollution to the environment. Interestingly, from another view on the composition of terylene, it is a good carbon source to prepare fluorescent CDs directly and provides a new idea for the recycling of waste fabrics.

In this work, we have synthesized chitosan non-woven fabric modified with fluorescent CDs from terylene to achieve the efficient detection and adsorption of Cr(VI) (Scheme 1 ). The novel CDs were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method used dyed terylene with red 167 and urea as precursors. By condensation of amino and carboxyl groups, the CDs were modified on a wet-spun chitosan non-woven fabric. This functionalized fabric CNF-TCDs3 showed high selectivity and sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection and the limit of detection and response interval were determined (The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.15 mg/L). In addition, the adsorption performance of CNF-TCDs3 to Cr(VI) was evaluated. The fluorescence response and efficient adsorption mechanisms were discussed. Moreover, the CNF-TCDs3 could be further used to real-time and semi-quantitatively detect Cr(VI) on a smartphone-integrated-device and efficiently remove Cr(VI) in real samples.

Pure polyester is provided by Dongguan Zhicheng Fiber Products Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Red 167 is provided by Suzhou Kaihe Mingxing New Material Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). The dyed waste polyester fabric was provided by Key Laboratory of Ecological Dyeing& Finishing, Wuhan Textile University. (Hubei, China). Chitosan non-woven fabric is made by wet spinning. Ethylene glycol, urea, K2Cr2O7, KCl, AlCl3, CaCl2, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Na2CO3, NaHCO4, Na2SO4, Na3PO4, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, Na2SiO3, NaNO3, Na2WO4, Na2MoO4 and CrCl3·6H2O were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NSH) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China); HgCl2 was obtained from Guizhou Tongren Yinhu Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guizhou, China). All reagents are of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The TCDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using terylene dyed azo dye Red 167 and urea as raw materials. In detail, 0.5 g of terylene dyed with red 167 was dispersed in 10 mL of ethylene glycol solution. Then, 0.5 g of urea was poured into the dispersion with further stirring for 15 min. The mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined and heated at 200 °C for 12 h, then naturally cooled down to room temperature. The solution was purified by centrifugation at 12,000 r/min and filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous filter. After that, the solid TCDs were obtained by the solution drying in the vacuum oven at 80 °C, and further re-dispersed into deionized water. Finally, the water-soluble TCDs (7% wt) was stored at 4 °C for later use.

The CNF-TCDs were prepared according to the reported method [34]. The chitosan non-woven fabrics modified by TCDs solution with a series of concentration (0.2% wt, 0.4% wt, 0.6% wt, 0.8% wt, 1% wt), and further washed with deionized water until the eluent was non-fluorescent, and placed in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 2 h. The prepared samples were named as CNF-TCDs1, CNF-TCDs2, CNF-TCDs3, CNF-TCDs4, CNF-TCDs5.

To test the selectivity of TCDs towards Cr(VI), 20 μL of purified TCDs solution was added to 3 mL Cr(VI), Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, CO3 2–, HCO4 -, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, SiO3 2-, NO3 –, WO4 2-, MoO4 2- (20 mg/L) solutions with 10 s shaken. To determine the detection limit of TCDs for Cr(VI), 20 μL of TCDs solution was added into 3 mL of Cr(VI) with concentration of 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15 mg/L, respectively. Then, all the emission intensity was tested on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL-2500, the voltage 700 V, slits 5 nm, 5 nm).

All the prepared CNF-TCDs were tailored into a size of 1 cm × 1 cm. CNF-TCDs3 were chosen to examine the selectivity to Cr(VI). CNF-TCDs3 were put into 3 mL 20 mg/L Cr(VI), Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Fe3+ CO3 2–, HCO4 -, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, SiO3 2-, NO3 –, WO4 2-, MoO4 2- solutions, then soaked for 5 min, and dried at 60 °C for 2 h in a vacuum oven. To determine the detection limit of CNF-TCDs3 for Cr(VI), CNF-TCDs3 were added into 3 mL of Cr(VI) concentration of 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15 mg/L, respectively. Then, all the emission intensity was tested on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL-2500, the voltage 700 V, slits 5 nm, 5 nm) under 368 nm excitation. All the images were captured by a Xiaomi smartphone on an apparatus we have reported before [38] and then processed by MATLAB software.

The adsorption of CNF and CNF-TCDs3 towards Cr(VI) were determined by batch experiments. In adsorption kinetics, CNF-TCDs3 was added to a 20 mg/L Cr(VI) solution, and the remaining concentration at different time (1, 2, 5, 8, 15, and 30 min) was measured by ICP-OES to calculate the adsorption capacity. The pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and intraparticle diffusion model equations were used for kinetic fitting. In isotherm adsorption, CNF-TCDs3 were put into different concentration of Cr(VI) solution (50, 80, 110, 140, 180, 220, 300, 400 mg/L) until adsorption reached equilibrium. The Langmuir, Freundlish and Sips isotherm adsorption models were used to fit the adsorption isotherm curve. The adsorption experiment of CNF to Cr(VI) was the same as above (The calculation formula is in the supporting information S1-S7).

The recyclability of CNF-TCDs3 was examined by repeated adsorption and desorption experiments. Firstly, 10 mg CNF-TCDs3 were used to adsorb 50 mL Cr(VI) (20 mg/L) until the adsorption reached equilibrium, and then CNF-TCDs3 were eluted with 20 mL NaOH solution and washed with deionized water. The changes in fluorescence and color were separately observed after adsorption.

Firstly, the 2 mm × 2 mm CNF-TCDs3 was immersed in the solution with the concentration of Cr(VI) of 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14 mg/L, respectively for 1 min. Then they were spread in a self-made grid groove after drying. The fluorescent brightness colorimetric device was prepared according to our previous work [35]. The same size CNF-TCDs3 was immersed in the unknown solution, and placed in the center of the colorimetric device after drying. The approximate concentration of Cr(VI) in the unknown solution was judged by contrast brightness. In addition, the CNF-TCDs3 was used to remove Cr(VI) in the simulated industrial wastewater. The actual test water sample was a self-made simulated water sample, which contains 1 ppm of Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, Fe3+, NO3 – ions. 50 mg of CNF-TCDs3 was filled into a glass tube with a hole in the bottom and compacted to make a filter. Then, 20 mL the industrial wastewater with a Cr(VI) concentration of 20 mg/L was poured into the filter and let the wastewater filter naturally. The change in the fluorescence intensity of the adsorbent material was captured to analyze the degree of adsorption. Based on this simple device, a simulated industrial adsorption tower device was built to remove Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater. The effluent was collected, and the total chromium content was measured by ICP.

The size and distribution of the CDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM Hitachi Japan) and laser particle size analyzer (DLS NanoS90 Malvern). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the material was tested by ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV TU-1810 Persee China). The fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence detection spectrum of the material were tested by fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL FL-2500 Hitachi Japan). The functional group structure of the material was analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR TENSOR27 Bruker). The total chromium content in the solution was tested on inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer. (ICP-OES, ICP20607 Skyray). The crystalline structure of the TCDs was characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD D/max2500 Rigaku Japan). The morphology and element composition of the material were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM SU8000 Hitachi Japan). The element ratio was analyzed and characterized by element analysis (Elemantar: Vario UNICUBE). The element valence was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS Thermo ESCALAB 250Xl US). All photos were taken via the smartphone (Redmi K40 Xiaomi China).

The morphology, size and distribution of TCDs were characterized through TEM and DLS. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) exhibited that the TCDs was nearly spherical particles with good dispersibility. The average particle diameter is 4.7 ± 0.1 nm, which was consistent with the particle size distribution of the DLS characterized (Fig. 1b). The optical properties of TCDs were performed through UV–vis and FL spectra. (Fig. 1S) Two UV–vis absorption peaks at 238 nm and 300 nm were observed, which attributed to the π-π* transition of the C = C bond and the n-π* transition of the C = O/C = N bonds in TCDs [28,36,37]. As observed, the fluorescence spectra showed that the maximum FL emission peak of TCDs was at 490 nm and the corresponding best excitation peak was at 368 nm. Furthermore, TCDs exhibited a green FL under 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 1S), which agreed with FL spectra. The fluorescence stability of the TCDs was also studied in Fig. S2. With the aging time of the TCDs, the fluorescence intensity changes slightly, which proved that the TCDs have good stability.

The chitosan non-woven fabric was modified with TCDs by amidation reaction [34]. A various amount of TCDs were modified on the CNF, and the results showed (Fig. 1c) that the fluorescence brightness of CNF-TCDs material became stronger and then remain unchanged with the added amount of TCDs increasing. When the amount of TCDs reached to 0.6% wt, the binding sites on the chitosan non-woven fabric were almost completely occupied. Therefore, CNF-TCDs3 was selected for the following experiments. In addition, the effect of acidity and alkalinity on the fluorescence performance of CNF-TCDs3 was studied (Fig. S3). It was observed that there was no fluorescence in the pH range of 1 to 2, and the fluorescence intensity of CNF-TCDs3 maintained relatively stabilized when the pH range was 3 to 13. The former was attributed to the dissolution of the chitosan non-woven fabric under strong acid condition. The results showed that CNF-TCDs3 was suitable for the detection of Cr(VI) in practical water solutions. Compared with TCDs, the maximum FL excitation peak of CNF-TCDs3 had a red shift from 368 nm to 383 nm, and the emission spectra had a blue shift from green at 490 nm to light at 440 nm (Fig. S4). It was mainly due to the carbon dots fixed on the chitosan non-woven fabric after modification. The dipole moment of the excited state is reduced, and the energy loss to change the dipole moment was weakened either, then resulting in a blue shift of the fluorescence [38]. In order to confirm this conclusion, a fluorescence test of TCDs was performed in water, ethylene glycol, methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol solvents. The spectrums were shown in Fig. S5, as the solvent polarity decreases, the fluorescence emission spectrum gradually blue shifts.

The chemical compositions of TCDs, CNF, and CNF-TCDs3 were characterized through FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1d, all broad peaks at 3300 cm−1-3500 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. In TCDs, there was an C = O stretching vibration peak of amide (–CO-NH-) at 1670 cm−1, and three peaks at 1600 cm−1,1500 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 were attributed to skeleton vibrations of the aromatic ring [39]. It proved that there was a reaction between –CO- of polyester and –NH2 of urea during the formation of CDs. For CNF and CNF-TCDs3, the two peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibrations of –NH2 and N–H on chitosan [40,41]. It was notable that CNF-TCDs3 had a new splitting peak at 1630 cm−1, which might be due to the amidation of the amino group in chitosan and the carboxyl group in TCDs. In summary, TCDs was successfully modified on chitosan non-woven fabric through amidation reaction.

The network structures and morphologies of the CNF and CNF-TCDs3 could be clearly found in SEM images. The CNF-TCDs3 were staggered to form a network structure (Fig. 1e), which was a typical non-woven loose fiber structure. Comparing with the CNF (Fig. S6), the network structure of the fiber was unchanged after modified of the TCDs. The elemental composition of the CNF and CNF-TCDs3 were analyzed using EDX (Fig. S7-S8) and elemental analysis (Table S1). The proportion of C increased after modifying with TCDs, which might be attributed to the rich carbon content in the TCDs. The XRD patterns of the TCDs, CNF and CNF-TCDs3 were shown in Fig. 1f. For TCDs, a broad diffraction peak with 23.2° as the prominent 2θ value was observed, which depicted the (002) lattice spacing corresponding to a d-spacing value of around 0.37 nm. The results were shown that it was mainly amorphous carbon, and the structure might be in form of graphene and hexagonal graphite structures [42–44]. Both CNF and CNF-TCDs3 showed a characteristic peaks at 20.1°, which was attributed to the amorphous structure of chitosan [45]. Additionally, multiple peaks at 25.9°, 31.7°, 34.3° and 36.1° were obtained, which might be attributed to the formation of some crystal structures after the reaction of chitosan non-woven fabric with TCDs.

To evaluate the efficiency of CNF-TCDs3 for Cr(VI) detection, a series of experiments were carried out. As the increase of Cr(VI) concentration, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased until it was completely quenched (Fig. S9). The fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Cr(VI) ranged from 0 to 15 mg/L (Fig. 2 a), and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.15 mg/L (R2 = 0.98) (the formula was provided by supporting information S8). This is much lower than the reported multifunctional materials [46–48]. Moreover, the fluorescence images were shown the quenching process of CNF-TCDs3 to Cr(VI) more intuitively (inset of Fig. 2b). Through the simulation calculation of the fluorescence brightness of the real images (the formula was provided by supporting information S9), the fluorescence brightness and the concentration also had a good linear relationship, which could also be used as a means of quantitative detection by taking images. In addition, the detection of Cr(VI) was compared with TCDs (Fig. S10a-b), the LOD of TCDs was 0.43 mg/L. The modified chitosan non-woven fabric had lower LOD, and more sensitive to Cr(VI).

Selectivity of CNF-TCDs3 to different cations and oxyanions especially the possible interfering ions were performed. From the F/F0 histogram in Fig. 2c-d, the ratio of F/F0 was lower than 0.2 when Cr(VI) was present. When CNF-TCDs3 detected other ions, the values of F/F0 were closed to 1. Meanwhile, the coexistence of ions was also considered, and the results were consistent. This indicated that Cr(VI) had obvious quenching effect on the fluorescence of CNF-TCDs3, while other ions almost had no response. The anti-interference tests were also carried out, when mix ions with poor selectivity, the CNF-TCDs3 also exhibited high selectivity to Cr(VI) (Fig. 2c-d). Similarly, the selectivity of pure TCDs to Cr(VI) was also tested.(Fig. S10c-d) It was obvious that TCDs was interfered by Fe3+. For Fe3+, many studies have shown that it was easy to statically or dynamically quench the fluorescence of CDs [49,50]. Being modified on the chitosan non-woven fabric, the interference of Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity was overcome. This was mainly due to the weak adsorption capacity of CNF-TCDs3 for Fe3+. The novel synthetic materials CNF-TCDs3 showed excellent selectivity.

The kinetic parameters of the CNF-TCDs3 for adsorption of Cr(VI) were calculated according to the pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model (Fig. 3 a). It was obvious that the adsorption equilibrium time was only 8 min, which was much shorter than the reported adsorption materials [46,47]. In addition, the correlation R2 of the pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model were all above 0.99, indicating that both physical adsorption and chemical adsorption existed in the adsorption process. The impact of TCDs on the adsorption process of CNF-TCDs3 was examined. As shown in Fig. S11a, the modification of TCDs hardly affected the adsorption equilibrium time. The adsorption isotherm curves of the CNF-TCDs3 for Cr(VI) were fitted according to the Langmuir, Freundlich and Sips isotherm models. (Fig. 3b) The results showed that the CNF-TCDs3 isotherm adsorption was more in line with the Langmuir model, the correlation R2 = 0.988, and the maximum saturated adsorption capacity was 203.0 mg/g. The isothermal adsorption of pure CNF was still more in line with the Langmuir model (Fig. S11b), and the maximum saturated adsorption capacity of CNF was 238.5 mg/g, which was slightly larger than that of CNF-TCDs3. This might be caused by the modification of TCDs, which occupied a certain cavity and adsorption sites. Nevertheless, the CNF-TCDs3 still had a good adsorption capacity for Cr(VI). Compared with other chitosan-based adsorption materials, the CNF-TCDs3 still had a competitive adsorption capacity for Cr(VI) (Table S2).

The influence of common electrolytes such as NaCl, NaNO3, Na2SO4, Na2CO3, Ca(NO3)2 on the adsorption of Cr(VI) by CNF-TCDs3 has been studied. As shown in Fig. S12, when these electrolytes have the same concentration of Cr(VI) as 20 mg/L and the added amount of CNF-TCDs3 was 10 mg, the electrolyte only slightly interferes with the adsorption of Cr(VI) by the material. Among them, sulfate had the greatest interference, which may be due to the fact that SO4 2- can crosslink with protonated amino groups, reducing the number of CNF-TCDs3 binding sites [41].

The adsorption–desorption performance of CNF-TCDs3 was studied. The histogram showed (Fig. 3c) that the saturated adsorption capacity of CNF-TCDs3 almost unchanged after five-cycle experiments, indicating that the material exhibited good reusability. At the same time, it had a very high desorption capacity, and the average desorption rate of five desorption was greater than 90%. The desorption rate was also studied in Fig. S13, and it was obtained that the desorption equilibrium can be reached in less than 1 min, which shows that Cr(VI) could be rapid and efficient desorbed by the NaOH. After adsorption, it was obviously that the color of CNF-TCDs3 changed from white to yellow (Fig. 3d), which confirmed Cr(VI) adsorbed on CNF-TCDs3. Using 0.1 M NaOH as a desorbent, the color of CNF-TCDs3 returned to white. After 5 cycles repeated experiments, the color of CNF-TCDs3 became dark, which might be the residual Cr(VI) after multiple cycles. The process of adsorption and desorption could influence fluorescence brightness. After several repetitions, the fluorescence brightness becomes darker, which attributed to the incomplete desorption of Cr(VI). The CNF-TCDs3 had potential to be utilized in the actual application process.

As displayed in Fig. S14a, the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) had a broad peak at 300 nm-500 nm, and the fluorescence spectrum showed that the optimum excitation and emission peaks of CNF-TCDs3 were at 383 nm and 440 nm, respectively. The fluorescence spectrum of CNF-TCDs3 overlapped with the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI), indicating that the fluorescence quenching might be caused by inner filter effect (IFE) [21,27,51]. In order to confirm this detection mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime before and after the detection of Cr(VI) was tested. As shown in Figure S14b, the average fluorescence lifetime of TCDS was 4.40 ns, while the average fluorescence lifetime of TCDS + Cr(VI) was 4.14 ns. The detection mechanism was further confirmed as internal filtration effect.

The SEM-EDX, BET, FTIR, XPS and Zeta potential tests were carried out to study the adsorption mechanism of CNF-TCDs3 for Cr(VI). The SEM images (Fig. S15a) showed that the CNF-TCDs3 has a large number of interlaced fibers, which provided many sites for adsorption. The BET was also utilized to characterize the specific surface area and pore size of CNF-TCDs3. The results showed (Table S3) that CNF-TCDs3 have a small specific surface area and pore size. It could be found from the EDX diagrams (Fig. S15b-f) that after adsorption the composition of CNF-TCDs3 not only contains C, N, and O elements but Cr, indicating that Cr(VI) was uniformly adsorbed on CNF-TCDs3. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4 a) appeared a new peak near 800 cm−1 after adsorption, which attributed to the vibration of Cr-O-Cr after adsorption [52]. The FTIR spectrum after adsorption showed that the peak at 1630 cm−1 was decreased and very sharp, which was attributed to the protonation of the amino group on the CNF-TCDs3. The changes in the range of 1400 cm−1-1500 cm−1 also illustrated the process of amino protonation. The Zeta potential test was also carried out, as shown in Fig. 4b, when pH = 6.5, CNF and CNF-TCDs3 were positively charged. These results indicated that the possible adsorption mechanism of CNF-TCDs3 was that the contained amino groups were protonated and positively charged, and the acid radical ions of Cr(VI) were adsorbed by electrostatic attraction. This result was consistent with the reported adsorption of Cr(VI) by chitosan [41]. In addition, due to the weak negative charge of TCDs, the positive charge of CNF-TCDs3 weakened, which might cause the maximum saturated adsorption capacity of CNF-TCDs3 decreased.

The XPS spectrum showed the peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s in Fig. 4c. Comparing with the XPS spectrum before adsorption, two new peaks of Cr(VI) appeared, which proved the existence of Cr(VI). Then, the XPS spectrum was subdivided into Cr2p, N1s and O1s spectra. For high-resolution Cr2p spectrum (Fig. 4d), there were two peaks at 576.6 eV and 586.4 eV, which correspond to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Cr, respectively. Among them, the binding energys at 576.6 eV and 586.0 eV were related to Cr(III) and Cr(VI). This indicated that during the adsorption of Cr(VI), Cr(VI) was partially reduced to Cr(III) (calculated as 67.43% according to the peak area of Fig. 4d) and the reduced Cr(III) ions were also adsorbed by CNF-TCDs3 [46]. In addition, the effect of adsorbed Cr(III) on the adsorption of Cr(VI) by CNF-TCDs3 was also studied. As shown in the Fig. S16, the same concentration of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) were adsorbed in turn, and CNF-TCDs3 adsorbed a small amount of Cr(III) to increase the adsorption capacity of Cr(VI). This may be due to the positive charge of Cr(III), which helped CNF-TCDs3 adsorbing dichromate ions. The N1s spectrum after adsorption were shown in Fig. 8e. A new binding energy of N1s at 401.3 eV appeared, which was attributed to the –NH3 + [53]. This confirms that amino groups were protonated, which was consistent with the FTIR results. Meantime, the binding energy of O1s moved from 532 eV to 532.3 eV (Fig. 4f), indicating that the –OH was also involved in the adsorption. Based on the data characterized in FTIR, XPS, and Zeta potential, the adsorption mechanism was confirmed to be the protonated positively charged amino groups, and Cr(VI) was adsorbed through electrostatic attraction. The adsorption of Cr(III) might be the coordination of hydroxyl groups.

The actual test water sample was a self-made simulated water sample, which contains 1 ppm of Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, Fe3+, NO3 – ions. The CNF-TCDs3 was used to simulate the detection and adsorption of the Cr(VI) in real wastewater. First, the concentration of Cr(VI) in the simulated industrial wastewater was semi-quantitatively detected through a self-made detection device (Fig. 5 a). The details of the device (Fig. S17) can refer to our previous work [35]. The images were captured by a smartphone. It can be estimated by the human eye (Fig. 5b) that the concentration of Cr(VI) in the unknown solution was in the range of 2 ∼ 4 mg/L. Compared with portable spectrophotometers, our material and method don't need expensive optical elements such as gratings. Imaging colorimetric detection can be realized through lens, dichroic mirror and UV LED, and the whole cost is very low. During on-site test, semi quantitative detection can be realized conveniently and within one minute. If we expand the colorimetric card, we can realize the semi quantitative detection of multiple samples at the same time. The wastewater sample was also tested by ICP-OES to verify the accuracy of semi-quantitative testing. The concentration of Cr(VI) was measured to be 3 mg/L on ICP-OES, which confirms the feasibility of semi-quantitative testing.

Then, the Cr(VI) in the simulated industrial wastewater was removed through a self-made simple device. The simple device was shown in Fig. 5c, 50 mg CNF-TCDs3 were filled in a glass device with a hole at the bottom, 20 mL of Cr(VI) (20 mg/L) wastewater was poured into the glass device and dropped freely. It could be clearly seen the yellow color solution became a colorless one after adsorption. The whole process could be found in the Video S1. Meanwhile, Fig. S18 showed that the fluorescence of CNF-TCDs3 was gradually quenched (Video S2). Based on this simple device, a simulated industrial adsorption tower device was built to remove Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater (Fig. S19a-b). Finally, the filtrate was collected, and the concentration of total chromium was measured to be 0.21 mg/L on ICP-OES, which was lower than allowable industrial discharge standard. In summary, this multifunctional material could efficient utilization in practical life and has important practical use value.

In summary, we have developed a dual-functional fluorescent fabrics CNF-TCDs3 for efficient detection and removal of Cr(VI). A novel fluorescent CDs originated from waste polyester fabric were modified on chitosan non-woven fabric which were prepared by the wet spinning method. This functionalized fabrics CNF-TCDs3 had good fluorescence stability in water. It exhibited specific selective detection and efficient removal for Cr(VI). The LOD was 0.15 mg/L, which was much lower than the reported dual-functional materials for the removal of Cr(VI). The maximum adsorption capacity of CNF-TCDs3 for Cr(VI) was 203.0 mg/g, which was higher than that of many chitosan-based adsorption materials. This functionalized fabrics CNF-TCDs3 could be recycled for five times and still maintain a high relative removal rate (above 92%). Furthermore, the CNF-TCDs3 could successfully applied to semi-quantitatively detect Cr(VI) on a smartphone-integrated-device and efficiently remove Cr(VI) in real samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the fund of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803065). The authors would like to thank Lang Jiang from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS, BET analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.134202.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
